turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 22-179, United States v. Hansen.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: The federal government convicted Helaman Hansen under a statute that prohibits encouraging or inducing unlawful immigration. The Ninth Circuit held that this provision is overbroad and violation of the First Amendment's Free Speech Clause. The gist of the argument goes like this. When encourage and induce are given their ordinary meetings they encompass too much protected speech. For example, a political rally that expresses support for undocumented immigrants might have the effect of encouraging a non-citizen to reside here without authorization. Same for an op-ed protesting immigration policy or even a college counselor giving an undocumented student advice about scholarship opportunities. The Ninth Circuit was persuaded that the statute reaches this kind of speech, but we are not. Hansen asks the Court to give the words encourage and induce the meaning they would have in everyday speech. But in criminal law, encourage and induce have been used for hundreds of years as terms of art that refer to the crimes of solicitation and aiding and abetting. That is how Congress use them here and when encourage and induce are given their criminal law meanings the over-breadth problem falls away. The statute has many lawful applications to non-expressive conduct, such as smuggling non-citizens into the country, providing counterfeit immigration documents and arranging fraudulent marriages. Applications like these do not implicate the First Amendment at all. To the extent that the provision reaches any speech, the normal limits of solicitation and facilitation liability confined its application to speech integral to criminal conduct. And that category of speech is unprotected. We do not hold that the encouraging or inducing provision will be constitutional in every future application. There's no way we could predict that. We hold simply that the provision's broad legitimate sweep does not permit us to apply the strong medicine of First Amendment over-breadth. In future cases, litigants remained free to argue that the statute is unconstitutional as applied to particular facts. The judgment of the Ninth Circuit is reversed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Sotomayor joins.
